title: Winfred Dotse-Gborgbortsi
text:
My work is grounded in the belief that better data leads to better health outcomes. By combining GIS and public health methods, I examine where and why inequalities in healthcare access exist, particularly for women and children. My research supports data-driven planning, helping decision-makers allocate resources more equitably and improve health service delivery.
My research sits at the crossroads of geographic data science and public health, with a particular interest in how location and distribution of quality health services influence health outcomes. I focus on:
 Healthcare accessibility and utilisation
 Maternal, newborn, and child health
 Spatial health inequalities
 Routine health data systems
 Spatial epidemiology and mapping disease patterns

title: Research
text:
Research and Engagement Impact Testimonial



“The Countdown Fellows GIS training and R mapping app have been transformative in building analytical capacity. These initiatives have empowered Fellows to generate and share actionable insights, enhancing data-driven decision-making in maternal and child health. Their impact extends beyond individual growth to fostering broader knowledge exchange and collaboration across countries.”

 Maternal, Newborn and Child Health

 Spatial Inequaliteis

 Routine Health Data Analysis


My research sits at the crossroads of geographic data science and public health, with a particular interest in how location and distribution of quality health services influence health outcomes. I focus on:
 Healthcare accessibility and utilisation
 Maternal, newborn, and child health
 Spatial health inequalities
 Routine health data systems
 Spatial epidemiology and mapping disease patterns

title: Winfred Dotse-Gborgbortsi
text:
Current Projects
I’m currently involved in several collaborative research initiatives, including:

Countdown to 2030 – A multi-country effort working with health ministries and academic partners across more than 26 African nations. We use routine health facility data to track progress towards key health-related Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).
CONTAIN Project– As a Co-Investigator on this UKRI-funded project, I work alongside a cross-disciplinary team exploring new ways to understand and manage the spread of infectious diseases through spatial and social analysis.

title: Winfred Dotse-Gborgbortsi
text:
Collaborations & Impact
I regularly collaborate with government health agencies, NGOs, and international partners to turn research into real-world solutions. My work has informed health strategies across low- and middle-income countries, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa. My PhD, funded by the ESRC, was recognised nationally and locally for its impact on evidence-based policymaking and capacity development in Ghana.
Below is a video produced by the ESRC highlighting my research impact in Ghana.

title: Winfred Dotse-G
text:
Generating actionable insights for decisions to improve health

  
    

    
  
    
     twitter
  
  
    
     Github
  

  
  
I’m a multidisciplinary academic specialising in spatial epidemiology at University College London, with over 15 years of experience in public health intelligence and research.
My career spans both academia and public health practice. I’ve held a Senior Research Fellowship with the WorldPop Research Group at the University of Southampton, and previously worked with the Ghana Health Service as a Public Health Officer. I also led monitoring and evaluation at Good Neighbors Ghana.
My work focuses on using data and spatial analysis to inform public health decisions and policy.

title: More about me…
text:
I am a Lecturer in Geographies of Health at University College London, specialising in spatial epidemiology and maternal health in low- and middle-income countries. My work sits at the intersection of public health, geospatial analysis, and social research, with a focus on generating actionable insights to improve health systems and outcomes.
Across academia and public health practice, I am committed to producing high-quality, policy-relevant research and supporting the next generation of scholars and practitioners in global health and geospatial data science.

title: More about me…
text:
Education






Note





PhD, Human Geography (Spatial Epidemiology) – University of Southampton
Thesis: Spatial patterns of birthing care utilisation in Ghana
MSc, Applied GIS & Remote Sensing (Distinction) – University of Southampton
MSc, Social Research Methods (Distinction) – University of Southampton
BSc, Public Health (First Class Honours) – University of Ghana
Diploma, Health Information Management – College of Health, Kintampo (Best Student)
Postgraduate Certificate in Academic Practice – Fellow of the Higher Education Academy

title: More about me…
text:
Work Experience
My professional experience spans research, teaching, monitoring and evaluation, and public health practice. I currently serve as a Lecturer in Geographies of Health at University College London, following previous appointments at the University of Southampton as a Senior Research Fellow, Research Fellow, and Senior Research Assistant. Prior to my academic career in the UK, I worked within the Ghana Health Service, serving as a Public Health Officer at Kibi Government Hospital and as the District Health Information Officer for the Atiwa District. I also led monitoring and evaluation work for Good Neighbors Ghana, where I contributed to programme effectiveness and community-based health interventions.

title: More about me…
text:
Impact and Awards
My contributions to research and impact have been recognised through several awards. I am the winner of the South Coast Doctoral Training Partnership ESRC Impact Prize and the runner-up for the National ESRC Early Career Impact Prize in the UK. I have also received the Doctoral College Director’s Award for Engagement and Impact. My academic achievements have been supported through competitive funding, including an Economic and Social Research Council 1+3 scholarship and a Commonwealth Scholarship to study at the University of Southampton. During my postgraduate studies, I was honoured four times times with the Dean’s List award for outstanding academic performance.
Across academia and public health practice, I remain committed to producing high-quality research, driving real-world impact, and supporting the next generation of scholars and practitioners in global health and geospatial analysis.

title: Teaching, Knowledge Exchange and Health Systems Strengthening
text:
Teaching Testimonial



“Winfred has been an invaluable co-lead for our module for the last two years. His input has both broadened and deepened the content of the module, bringing unique insights from his work in Ghana, enriching the teaching by directly bringing his research into the education context. He teaches clearly and engages the students very effectively”.


My commitment is to helping students and healthcare professionals build strong analytical, spatial, and conceptual skills, and to mentoring emerging researchers in health and geographical sciences.
I have extensive experience in university teaching, spanning undergraduate and postgraduate levels. My roles have included module leadership, curriculum design, delivery of lectures and seminars, developing GIS practicals, designing authentic assessments, providing feedback, and coordinating teaching and learning activities. I have also supervised MSc students in GIS, Remote Sensing, and Applied Statistics.
I have delivered a range of knowledge exchange and capacity-building trainings across Africa and the UK, focusing on socio-economic and geospatial equity in health. These include hands-on workshops in Stata, R, and QGIS for the analysis of routine health and complex survey data, with audiences ranging from small research fellow cohorts to large international conference attendees. Notable trainings include sessions on equity analysis in immunisation and maternal and child health in Côte d’Ivoire and Rwanda, analytical research trainings for Countdown research fellows in Senegal, Kenya, and Rwanda, and spatial analysis workshops in Ghana.
My teaching spans geography, spatial data science, and public health, reflecting the interdisciplinary nature of my academic work. In my current role at UCL, I teach Spatial Data Analysis and Foundations of Geography, where I support students in developing strong spatial analytical foundations and an understanding of core geographical concepts and methods.
Between 2019 and 2025, during my time at the University of Southampton, I taught across a broad range of undergraduate and postgraduate modules in GIS, global health, and population health. These included master’s-level modules such as Core Skills in GIS and Health Services Organisation and Evaluation, the latter of which I co-led. I also contributed to foundational and advanced GIS teaching, including Introductory GIS, Advanced GIS, and GIS for Health Analysis, providing students with both technical competence and applied analytical skills. I also contributed to public health teaching in global and population health, Reproductive Health, Global Public Health, and Practicing Human Geographical Research. In addition to classroom teaching, I have supervised master’s-level and undergraduate dissertations across applied quantitative and geospatial fields. I am currently supervising two PhD students.

title: Consultancy
text:
I provide analytical, spatial, and research expertise to support evidence-based decision-making in public health, humanitarian, and development contexts. My consultancy work focuses on data management, geographic analysis, impact evaluation, and technical research support.






Important




Upskilling teams in geospatial analysis
Designing and conducting quantitative or mixed methods research
Data analysis for research and public health projects
Developing interactive dashboards to communicate research findings or other forms of data



Key areas of experience include:
 Data management: Designing and implementing systems to improve data quality and streamline workflows.
 GIS training: Delivering practical GIS training to build organisational capacity.
 Impact evaluations: Supporting programme assessments and evidence of effectiveness.
 Geographic accessibility modelling: Analysing service access and coverage using spatial modelling.
 Technical report writing: Producing clear, actionable reports for programmes and policy.
 Survey data collection: Designing instruments, managing fieldwork, and ensuring high-quality data.

title: A Refreshed Webpage
text:
I created my first website with R during my PhD at the University of Southampton, where I researched spatial epidemiology an ESRC South Coast DTP scholar. While that page is still online, a lot has changed since then. I have graduated, got promoted to a senior research fellow and moved on from my role at Southampton, and grown considerably as a researcher and practitioner. I am now a lecturer in geographies of health at the University College London. A refresh is long overdue.
I don’t want my webpage to be like those academic profile photos that look suspiciously young, only for visitors to meet the person in real life and realise just how much time has passed. This new site is a more honest reflection of where I am now: a spatial epidemiologist with a deep interest in using geospatial tools to tackle real-world public health challenges.
My journey has taken me from practicing as a public health officer with the Ghana Health Service, to leading monitoring and evaluation for an international NGO, to publishing peer-reviewed research on geographic disparities in health access across low- and middle-income countries. All of that experience shapes how I think about data, place, and health, and it is what I hope to bring to this space.
One thing I consistently failed to do on the old site was write blog posts. I promise to do better this time. I will be sharing my thoughts on all things public health and geographic data science, from opinions on contemporary public health issues to practical approaches to spatial data analysis, particularly for public health intelligence. If you have ever wondered how maps and machine learning can help answer questions about maternal health, hygiene access, or health inequalities, you are in the right place.

title: A Refreshed Webpage
text:
Welcome — Woezor!
So, keep calm, grab your coffee, and enjoy the posts. I am glad you are here.
